title: Three Daily Anchors
subtitle: A ritual structure for women whose days feel formless and fast
excerpt: A simple practice of choosing three daily anchors: one morning, one midday, one evening. Each anchor is small and takes under two minutes. Together they change the texture of the day from shapeless to structured without adding to the to-do list.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education, drawing on the daily-rhythm literature and the slow-living tradition of intentional anchors. The ritual structure draws on the framework in MONEY Journal (Rebecca Page, 2025).

practice.whenToUse: Use when days feel shapeless or when you reach the evening unsure of how the day went. Set the anchors in the morning.

content.text: A daily ritual for the day that passes in a blur. Three anchors: one in the morning, one at midday, one in the evening. Each one takes under two minutes. The point is the marking, not the activity.

Each morning, choose three anchors for the day. Write them on paper or say them aloud. One for the morning, one for midday, one for the evening. They do not have to be the same anchors every day, though they can be.
I am ready to release the feeling that I have no control over how this day goes. I release it now. I release it now. I release it now.
I am ready to allow three small points of intention to shape this day. I allow them now. I allow them now. I allow them now.

What counts as an anchor: a cup of tea drunk slowly before the children wake, a five-minute walk at lunch, a specific thing you will do to close the working day. Small, real, yours. Not aspirational. Something you will actually do.
Today has shape. I gave it that.
Say this in the evening when you have done the three. Write it down if that helps. Then do it again tomorrow.

Written for homemade.education, drawing on the daily-rhythm tradition in slow-living and intentional-homemaking writing. The five-part structure draws on the ceremony shape in MONEY Journal (Rebecca Page, 2025).
